name: Aesthetic–usability effect
description: Users perceive attractive interfaces as more usable; use aesthetics to support usability (not replace it) and interpret research signals correctly.

metadata.intent: Apply the aesthetic–usability effect responsibly in design and user research

must_use: Use aesthetics to reduce friction perception for minor issues
must_not: Use visual polish as a substitute for broken task completion
conventions: In research, compare what users do vs what they say to detect bias.

phases.title: How to use the effect (without lying to yourself)

steps.description: Definition: people often judge beautiful interfaces as easier to use, even when inherent usability isn't better. This buys tolerance for minor issues; it doesn't forgive broken flows.
In usability research, watch for 'nice UI' comments masking failures. Probe with workflow questions: 'How easy was it to find X?' and validate by task outcomes.
Design implication: use visual clarity (typography, spacing, consistent components, feedback states) to lower cognitive load and increase perceived ease—while still fixing functional friction.
